content.text: Visual Search

Visual search finds images that are similar to a query — where the query can itself be an image, a text description, or a combination of both. Unlike keyword search over filenames or alt-text, visual search operates on learned semantic embeddings: an image of a red running shoe and the phrase "red sneakers" map to nearby points in the same embedding space, so a single nearest-neighbour lookup handles both queries.
CLIP (Contrastive Language–Image Pre-training) is the dominant backbone. Its key property is a
shared embedding space
for images and text: the cosine similarity between an image embedding and a text embedding measures semantic alignment, which is exactly what retrieval needs.

attrs.alt: Visual search architecture: offline index building (image corpus → CLIP encoder → embedding matrix → ANN index) and online query serving (text/image query → CLIP encoder → ANN search → ranked results)

content.text: Embedding Images with CLIP

The first step is to encode every image in the corpus once and store the resulting vectors. Images and queries live in the same ℓ2-normalised space, so cosine similarity reduces to an inner product — the fastest operation FAISS supports.

import torch
import clip
from PIL import Image
from pathlib import Path
import numpy as np

# Load model (available sizes: ViT-B/32, ViT-B/16, ViT-L/14, ViT-L/14@336px)
device = "cuda" if torch.cuda.is_available() else "cpu"
model, preprocess = clip.load("ViT-L/14", device=device)
model.eval()

def encode_images(image_paths: list[str], batch_size: int = 64) -> np.ndarray:
    """Encode a list of image paths → (N, 512) float32 array, ℓ2-normalised."""
    all_features = []
    for i in range(0, len(image_paths), batch_size):
        batch_paths = image_paths[i : i + batch_size]
        images = torch.stack([
            preprocess(Image.open(p).convert("RGB")) for p in batch_paths
        ]).to(device)
        with torch.inference_mode():
            features = model.encode_image(images)          # (B, 512)
            features = features / features.norm(dim=-1, keepdim=True)  # ℓ2-normalise
        all_features.append(features.cpu().float().numpy())
    return np.vstack(all_features)   # (N, 512)

def encode_text(queries: list[str]) -> np.ndarray:
    """Encode text queries → (Q, 512) float32 array, ℓ2-normalised."""
    tokens = clip.tokenize(queries, truncate=True).to(device)
    with torch.inference_mode():
        features = model.encode_text(tokens)               # (Q, 512)
        features = features / features.norm(dim=-1, keepdim=True)
    return features.cpu().float().numpy()

# Encode your image corpus once
image_paths = sorted(Path("images/").glob("*.jpg"))
image_paths = [str(p) for p in image_paths]
embeddings = encode_images(image_paths)   # (N, 512)
np.save("embeddings.npy", embeddings)
print(f"Encoded {len(image_paths)} images → shape {embeddings.shape}")
Building a FAISS Index
FAISS (Facebook AI Similarity Search) is the standard library for approximate nearest-neighbour (ANN) search over dense vectors. The right index type depends on your dataset size and RAM budget.

import faiss
import numpy as np

embeddings = np.load("embeddings.npy").astype("float32")  # (N, 512)
N, D = embeddings.shape

# --- Option 1: Exact search (good for ≤100K vectors, ground-truth) ---
index_flat = faiss.IndexFlatIP(D)        # inner product = cosine for ℓ2-normalised vectors
index_flat.add(embeddings)

# --- Option 2: IVF + flat quantiser (1M+ vectors, ~97% recall) ---
nlist = 1024                             # number of Voronoi cells
quantiser = faiss.IndexFlatIP(D)
index_ivf = faiss.IndexIVFFlat(quantiser, D, nlist, faiss.METRIC_INNER_PRODUCT)
index_ivf.train(embeddings)              # learn cluster centroids
index_ivf.add(embeddings)
index_ivf.nprobe = 64                   # cells to search at query time (recall vs speed)

# --- Option 3: HNSW (best recall/speed balance, more RAM) ---
M = 32                                   # graph connections per node
index_hnsw = faiss.IndexHNSWFlat(D, M, faiss.METRIC_INNER_PRODUCT)
index_hnsw.hnsw.efConstruction = 200    # build-time beam width
index_hnsw.hnsw.efSearch = 64          # query-time beam width
index_hnsw.add(embeddings)

# --- Option 4: IVF + Product Quantisation (100M+ scale, RAM-limited) ---
M_pq = 64                               # number of sub-quantisers
index_ivfpq = faiss.IndexIVFPQ(quantiser, D, nlist, M_pq, 8)  # 8 bits per sub-vector
index_ivfpq.train(embeddings)
index_ivfpq.add(embeddings)

# Save the chosen index
faiss.write_index(index_hnsw, "visual_search.index")
Serving Queries
At query time, encode the query with the same CLIP model (text encoder for text queries, vision encoder for image queries), run an ANN search, and join the top-K IDs with the metadata store.

import faiss
import numpy as np
import clip
import torch
from PIL import Image
import json

device = "cuda" if torch.cuda.is_available() else "cpu"
model, preprocess = clip.load("ViT-L/14", device=device)
model.eval()

# Load pre-built index and metadata
index = faiss.read_index("visual_search.index")
image_paths = json.load(open("image_paths.json"))   # list of file paths, same order as index

def search_by_text(query: str, k: int = 10):
    tokens = clip.tokenize([query], truncate=True).to(device)
    with torch.inference_mode():
        q = model.encode_text(tokens)
        q = q / q.norm(dim=-1, keepdim=True)
    q_np = q.cpu().float().numpy()                   # (1, 512)
    scores, ids = index.search(q_np, k)             # scores: (1,k), ids: (1,k)
    return [
        {"path": image_paths[i], "score": float(s)}
        for i, s in zip(ids[0], scores[0]) if i >= 0
    ]

def search_by_image(image_path: str, k: int = 10):
    image = preprocess(Image.open(image_path).convert("RGB")).unsqueeze(0).to(device)
    with torch.inference_mode():
        q = model.encode_image(image)
        q = q / q.norm(dim=-1, keepdim=True)
    q_np = q.cpu().float().numpy()
    scores, ids = index.search(q_np, k)
    return [
        {"path": image_paths[i], "score": float(s)}
        for i, s in zip(ids[0], scores[0]) if i >= 0
    ]

# Examples
print(search_by_text("red running shoes", k=5))
print(search_by_image("query_photo.jpg", k=5))
Combined Text + Image Queries
Because text and image embeddings live in the same space, you can interpolate them to express "find images like this photo but with a blue colourway" — a query that is impossible with keyword search.

def search_by_image_and_text(
    image_path: str, text: str, alpha: float = 0.7, k: int = 10
):
    """Blend image and text embeddings. alpha=1 → pure image, alpha=0 → pure text."""
    # Image embedding
    img = preprocess(Image.open(image_path).convert("RGB")).unsqueeze(0).to(device)
    with torch.inference_mode():
        img_emb = model.encode_image(img)
        img_emb = img_emb / img_emb.norm(dim=-1, keepdim=True)

    # Text embedding
    tokens = clip.tokenize([text], truncate=True).to(device)
    with torch.inference_mode():
        txt_emb = model.encode_text(tokens)
        txt_emb = txt_emb / txt_emb.norm(dim=-1, keepdim=True)

    # Weighted sum, then re-normalise
    combined = alpha * img_emb + (1 - alpha) * txt_emb
    combined = combined / combined.norm(dim=-1, keepdim=True)
    q_np = combined.cpu().float().numpy()

    scores, ids = index.search(q_np, k)
    return [
        {"path": image_paths[i], "score": float(s)}
        for i, s in zip(ids[0], scores[0]) if i >= 0
    ]

# Find images similar to a shoe photo, but in blue
results = search_by_image_and_text("shoe.jpg", "blue", alpha=0.6, k=10)
Production with Qdrant

For production deployments, a managed vector database such as Qdrant, Weaviate, or Pinecone handles persistence, filtering, and horizontal scaling. Qdrant is open-source and supports payload filters — e.g. "find similar images that are in stock and in category=shoes" — which FAISS alone cannot do efficiently.
Fine-Tuning CLIP for Domain-Specific Retrieval

Off-the-shelf CLIP is trained on web-scale image-caption pairs and generalises well across domains. For specialised domains (medical imaging, satellite imagery, product catalogs) fine-tuning on domain-specific positive pairs improves retrieval accuracy.

The standard fine-tuning approach keeps the contrastive loss and updates all or part of the model on domain pairs. A cheaper alternative — linear probing — only trains a small projection head on top of frozen CLIP features.

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import clip

device = "cuda"
model, preprocess = clip.load("ViT-L/14", device=device)

# Fine-tune: update only the vision encoder's last 4 layers and the projection
for name, param in model.named_parameters():
    param.requires_grad = False   # freeze everything first

# Unfreeze last 4 transformer blocks + projection
for name, param in model.visual.named_parameters():
    if "transformer.resblocks.2" in name or "ln_post" in name or "proj" in name:
        param.requires_grad = True

optimizer = torch.optim.AdamW(
    [p for p in model.parameters() if p.requires_grad],
    lr=1e-6, weight_decay=0.2
)

def contrastive_loss(image_features, text_features, logit_scale):
    logits = logit_scale * image_features @ text_features.T  # (B, B)
    labels = torch.arange(len(logits), device=logits.device)
    loss_i = nn.functional.cross_entropy(logits, labels)
    loss_t = nn.functional.cross_entropy(logits.T, labels)
    return (loss_i + loss_t) / 2

# Training loop (domain_loader yields (images, captions) batches)
for images, captions in domain_loader:
    images  = images.to(device)
    tokens  = clip.tokenize(captions, truncate=True).to(device)

    img_feat = model.encode_image(images)
    txt_feat = model.encode_text(tokens)
    img_feat = img_feat / img_feat.norm(dim=-1, keepdim=True)
    txt_feat = txt_feat / txt_feat.norm(dim=-1, keepdim=True)

    loss = contrastive_loss(img_feat, txt_feat, model.logit_scale.exp())
    optimizer.zero_grad()
    loss.backward()
    optimizer.step()
Evaluation: Recall@K and mAP

Retrieval quality is measured with Recall@K and Mean Average Precision (mAP). Recall@K measures whether the correct item appears in the top-K results; mAP rewards systems that rank correct items higher.

import numpy as np

def recall_at_k(query_embeddings, gallery_embeddings, ground_truth_ids, k=10):
    """
    query_embeddings: (Q, D) float32, ℓ2-normalised
    gallery_embeddings: (N, D) float32, ℓ2-normalised
    ground_truth_ids: list of sets, ground_truth_ids[i] = set of relevant gallery indices for query i
    """
    # Inner product = cosine similarity for ℓ2-normalised vectors
    scores = query_embeddings @ gallery_embeddings.T   # (Q, N)
    ranked = np.argsort(-scores, axis=1)               # (Q, N) descending

    recall_scores = []
    for i, rel_ids in enumerate(ground_truth_ids):
        top_k = set(ranked[i, :k].tolist())
        hits = len(top_k & rel_ids)
        recall_scores.append(hits / max(len(rel_ids), 1))
    return float(np.mean(recall_scores))

def mean_average_precision(query_embeddings, gallery_embeddings, ground_truth_ids, k=100):
    scores = query_embeddings @ gallery_embeddings.T
    ranked = np.argsort(-scores, axis=1)
    ap_scores = []
    for i, rel_ids in enumerate(ground_truth_ids):
        ap = 0.0
        n_hits = 0
        for rank, idx in enumerate(ranked[i, :k], start=1):
            if idx in rel_ids:
                n_hits += 1
                ap += n_hits / rank
        ap_scores.append(ap / max(len(rel_ids), 1))
    return float(np.mean(ap_scores))

# Example usage
print(f"Recall@10: {recall_at_k(q_emb, g_emb, gt_ids, k=10):.4f}")
print(f"mAP@100:   {mean_average_precision(q_emb, g_emb, gt_ids, k=100):.4f}")
Re-Ranking with a Cross-Encoder

ANN retrieval uses bi-encoders (each item encoded independently) which are fast but less accurate than cross-encoders that attend jointly over the query and candidate. A standard two-stage pipeline retrieves a large candidate set (top-100) cheaply with ANN, then re-ranks with a cross-encoder — e.g. BLIP-2 scoring image-text similarity — to improve precision at the top.

from transformers import BlipProcessor, BlipForImageTextRetrieval
from PIL import Image
import torch

# Stage 1: retrieve top-100 candidates with CLIP (fast)
candidates = search_by_text("red running shoes", k=100)

# Stage 2: re-rank with BLIP ITM (Image-Text Matching) cross-encoder
processor = BlipProcessor.from_pretrained("Salesforce/blip-itm-base-coco")
reranker  = BlipForImageTextRetrieval.from_pretrained("Salesforce/blip-itm-base-coco")
reranker.eval()

query = "red running shoes"
scored = []
for cand in candidates:
    image = Image.open(cand["path"]).convert("RGB")
    inputs = processor(images=image, text=query, return_tensors="pt")
    with torch.inference_mode():
        itm_score = reranker(**inputs, use_itm_head=True).itm_score
        score = torch.nn.functional.softmax(itm_score, dim=1)[:, 1].item()
    scored.append({**cand, "rerank_score": score})

# Sort by re-rank score
scored.sort(key=lambda x: x["rerank_score"], reverse=True)
print("Top-5 after re-ranking:")
for r in scored[:5]:
    print(f"  {r['path']}  CLIP={r['score']:.3f}  ITM={r['rerank_score']:.3f}")
Key Takeaways
CLIP's shared embedding space enables any-to-any retrieval
: text queries, image queries, and mixed queries all produce ℓ2-normalised 512-d vectors that can be compared with inner product — the same index serves all query types.
Choose your FAISS index by scale
: IndexFlatIP for exact search up to ~100K; IndexIVFFlat for millions of vectors; IndexHNSWFlat for best recall with fast queries; IndexIVFPQ when RAM is limited at 100M+ scale.
Embed the corpus offline, once
: encoding 1M images with CLIP ViT-L/14 on an A100 takes ~15 minutes. The resulting 2 GB float32 matrix is written once and queried at microsecond latency with FAISS.
Use vector databases (Qdrant, Pinecone) for production

: FAISS is a library, not a service. Production systems need persistence, payload filtering (by category, price, in-stock status), and horizontal scaling — all provided by purpose-built vector databases.
Two-stage pipelines beat single-stage at precision
: retrieve a large candidate set cheaply with ANN, then re-rank with an expensive cross-encoder (BLIP ITM). This gives cross-encoder accuracy at ANN speed.
Recall@K is the primary retrieval metric
: Recall@10 > 0.95 is achievable with IndexHNSWFlat and good embeddings. mAP captures whether relevant items rank at position 1 vs 10, which matters for e-commerce click-through.
